concepts.name: Development
concepts.definition: Development has many aspects. The purpose of this chapter is to enable students to understand this idea.

concepts.name: Economic Development
concepts.definition: Economic development can be measured and income is the most common method for measuring development.

concepts.name: Per Capita Income
concepts.definition: The average income which is the total income of the country divided by its total population.

concepts.name: Human Development Index
concepts.definition: HDI ranks in above table are out of 193 countries in all.

concepts.name: Sustainability of Development
concepts.definition: The question of sustainability of development raises many fundamentally new issues about the nature and process of development.

concepts.name: Renewable Resources
concepts.definition: Groundwater is an example of renewable resources. These resources are replenished by nature as in the case of crops and plants.

concepts.name: Non-Renewable Resources
concepts.definition: Non-renewable resources are those which will get exhausted after a few years of use.

concepts.name: Body Mass Index
concepts.definition: This is easy to calculate. Let each student in the class find out his or her weight and height.

concepts.name: Environmental Degradation
concepts.definition: Consequences of environmental degradation do not respect national or state boundaries.

concepts.name: Gross National Income
concepts.definition: Purchasing Power Parity that is used to calculate Gross National Income per capita in Table 1.6.

concepts.name: Infant Mortality Rate
concepts.definition: The number of children that die before the age of one year as a proportion of 1000 live children born in that particular year.

concepts.name: Literacy Rate
concepts.definition: The proportion of literate population in the 7-and-above age group.

concepts.name: Net Attendance Ratio
concepts.definition: The total number of children of age group of 15-17 years attending school as a percentage of total number of children in the same age group.

concepts.name: Public Distribution System
concepts.definition: The Public Distribution System (PDS) functions well.

concepts.name: Human Development Report
concepts.definition: The Human Development Report published by UNDP compares countries based on the educational levels of the people, their health status and per capita income.

concepts.name: Sri Lanka
concepts.definition: A small country in our neighbourhood, Sri Lanka, is much ahead of India in every respect and a big country like ours has such a low rank in the world.

concepts.name: Nepal
concepts.definition: Though Nepal and Bangladesh have low per capita income than that of India, yet they are better than India in life expectancy.

concepts.name: Bangladesh
concepts.definition: Though Nepal and Bangladesh have low per capita income than that of India, yet they are better than India in life expectancy.

concepts.name: India
concepts.definition: India comes in the category of low middle income countries because its per capita income in 2023 was just about US$ 10030 per annum.

concepts.name: United Nations Development Programme
concepts.definition: The Human Development Report published by UNDP compares countries based on the educational levels of the people, their health status and per capita income.

concepts.name: Economic Development
concepts.definition: A process of change in the economic life of people, as producers or consumers of goods and services.

concepts.name: Human Development Index (HDI)
concepts.definition: A measure of economic development that includes health, education, and income.

concepts.name: Primary Sector
concepts.definition: A sector of the economy that includes agriculture, forestry, and fishing.

concepts.name: Secondary Sector
concepts.definition: A sector of the economy that includes manufacturing and construction.

concepts.name: Tertiary Sector
concepts.definition: A sector of the economy that includes services such as finance, transportation, and communication.

concepts.name: Division of Labour
concepts.definition: A process where people specialize in different activities to increase productivity.

concepts.name: Globalisation
concepts.definition: A phenomenon that has influenced development and people around the world in various ways.

concepts.name: Consumer Rights
concepts.definition: The rights of citizens as consumers, including protection from business malpractices.

concepts.name: Credit
concepts.definition: The provision of funds to individuals or businesses for various purposes.

concepts.name: Money
concepts.definition: A medium of exchange that facilitates trade and commerce.

concepts.name: Banking
concepts.definition: An institution that provides financial services, including credit and deposit facilities.

concepts.name: Economic Indicators
concepts.definition: Statistics that measure economic activity, such as GDP and inflation.

concepts.name: GDP
concepts.definition: The total value of goods and services produced in a country.

concepts.name: Inflation
concepts.definition: A rise in the general price level of goods and services.

concepts.name: Employment
concepts.definition: The number of people working in a country or economy.

concepts.name: Unemployment
concepts.definition: The number of people who are not working in a country or economy.

concepts.name: Poverty
concepts.definition: A state of being poor, often measured by income or wealth.

concepts.name: Development Goals
concepts.definition: Objectives that aim to improve the quality of life of people.

concepts.name: SEZs
concepts.definition: Special Economic Zones, which are areas designated for economic development.

concepts.name: Globalisation and Development
concepts.definition: The relationship between globalisation and economic development.

concepts.name: Money as a medium of exchange
concepts.definition: Money acts as an intermediate in the exchange process, eliminating the need for double coincidence of wants.

concepts.name: Modern forms of money
concepts.definition: Currency - paper notes and coins, and deposits with banks.

concepts.name: Demand deposits
concepts.definition: Deposits that can be withdrawn on demand, sharing the essential features of money.

concepts.name: Cheque payments
concepts.definition: Payments made through cheques, allowing direct settlement without cash.

concepts.name: Banks and credit
concepts.definition: Banks mediate between surplus funds and loan requirements, charging higher interest rates.

concepts.name: Credit and its impact
concepts.definition: Credit can have a positive impact or create further problems for the borrower.

concepts.name: Formal and informal sources of credit
concepts.definition: Formal sources include banks and cooperatives, while informal sources include moneylenders and traders.

concepts.name: Terms of credit
concepts.definition: Interest rate, collateral, and documentation requirements, varying between credit arrangements.

concepts.name: Self-help groups
concepts.definition: Organising rural poor into groups for pooled savings and loans, reducing dependence on informal sources.

concepts.name: Grameen Bank
concepts.definition: A bank providing credit to the poor at reasonable rates, promoting development.

concepts.name: Double coincidence of wants
concepts.definition: A situation where a person desires to sell is exactly what the other wishes to buy.

concepts.name: Barter system
concepts.definition: A system where goods are directly exchanged without the use of money.

concepts.name: Collateral
concepts.definition: An asset used as a guarantee until the loan is repaid.

concepts.name: Interest rate
concepts.definition: The rate at which interest is charged on a loan.

concepts.name: Loan
concepts.definition: An agreement where the lender supplies the borrower with money, goods, or services in return for future payment.

concepts.name: Borrower
concepts.definition: A person who borrows money or goods from a lender.

concepts.name: Lender
concepts.definition: A person or institution that lends money or goods to a borrower.

concepts.name: Credit
concepts.definition: The ability to obtain goods or services before paying for them.

concepts.name: Money
concepts.definition: A medium of exchange, unit of account, and store of value.

concepts.name: Bank
concepts.definition: An institution that accepts deposits and provides loans to customers.

chapter_title: Chapter 4 Globalisation and the Indian Economy
subject: class_10_th_social_science_part_2

concepts.name: Globalisation
concepts.definition: The process of rapid integration of countries through greater foreign trade and foreign investment.

concepts.name: Multinational Corporations (MNCs)
concepts.definition: Companies that own or control production in more than one nation.

concepts.name: Foreign Investment
concepts.definition: Investment made by MNCs in other countries.

concepts.name: Foreign Trade
concepts.definition: The exchange of goods and services between countries.

concepts.name: Globalisation and the Indian Economy
concepts.definition: The impact of globalisation on the Indian economy.

concepts.name: Integration of Production and Markets
concepts.definition: The process of integrating production and markets across countries.

concepts.name: Factors that have enabled Globalisation
concepts.definition: Rapid improvement in technology, liberalisation of trade and investment policies, and pressures from international organisations.

concepts.name: Role of MNCs in Globalisation
concepts.definition: MNCs play a major role in the globalisation process by spreading their production and interacting with local producers.

concepts.name: Impact of Globalisation on Small Producers
concepts.definition: Globalisation has posed major challenges for small producers and workers.

concepts.name: Importance of Technology in Globalisation
concepts.definition: Technology, particularly IT, has played a big role in organising production across countries.

concepts.name: Liberalisation of Trade and Investment
concepts.definition: The removal of barriers to trade and investment.

concepts.name: Role of WTO in Globalisation
concepts.definition: WTO has put pressure on countries to liberalise their trade and investment policies.

concepts.name: Positive and Negative Effects of Globalisation
concepts.definition: Globalisation has created opportunities for paid work and higher standards of living for the well-off sections of society, but has also posed major challenges for small producers and workers.

concepts.name: Fair Globalisation
concepts.definition: Globalisation that creates opportunities for all and ensures that the benefits of globalisation are shared better.

concepts.name: Role of Government in Globalisation
concepts.definition: The government has a major role to play in making globalisation more 'fair'.

concepts.name: Importance of People's Organisations in Globalisation
concepts.definition: People's organisations must work together with the government to ensure that the benefits of globalisation are shared by all.

concepts.name: Globalisation and the Indian Economy
concepts.definition: The impact of globalisation on the Indian economy.

concepts.name: Consumer Movement

concepts.name: Consumer Awareness
concepts.definition: The awareness of consumers about their rights and responsibilities.

concepts.name: Consumer Protection Councils
concepts.definition: Organisations that guide consumers on how to file cases in Consumer Disputes Redressal Commissions.

concepts.name: Consumer Redressal Process

concepts.definition: The protection of consumers in India through laws and regulations.

concepts.name: Consumer Disputes

concepts.definition: The laws that protect consumers in India.

concepts.definition: The rights of consumers in the marketplace.

concepts.name: Gender Division
concepts.definition: A form of hierarchical social division seen everywhere, but rarely recognised in the study of politics.

concepts.name: Patriarchy
concepts.definition: A system that values men more and gives them power over women.

concepts.name: Sexual Division of Labour
concepts.definition: A system in which all work inside the home is either done by the women of the family, or organised by them through the domestic helpers.

concepts.name: Feminism
concepts.definition: A movement that aims to achieve equality between men and women.

concepts.name: Communalism
concepts.definition: A form of politics that is based on the belief that religion is the principal basis of social community.

concepts.name: Secularism
concepts.definition: A principle that separates religion from politics and ensures equal treatment of all religions.

concepts.name: Caste System
concepts.definition: A system in which people are divided into different castes based on their birth and occupation.

concepts.name: Caste Inequality
concepts.definition: A form of inequality that is based on the caste system and affects people's access to resources and opportunities.

concepts.name: Urbanisation
concepts.definition: A process in which people move from rural areas to urban areas.

concepts.name: Occupational Mobility
concepts.definition: A process in which people change their occupation from one generation to another.

concepts.name: Caste Hierarchy
concepts.definition: A system in which people are ranked according to their caste and occupation.

concepts.name: Social and Religious Diversity of India
concepts.definition: A concept that refers to the diversity of social and religious groups in India.

concepts.name: Panchayati Raj
concepts.definition: A system of local self-government in India.

concepts.name: Women's Reservation Act
concepts.definition: A law that reserves seats for women in legislative bodies.

concepts.name: Nari Shakti Vandan Adhiniyam
concepts.definition: A law that provides for the reservation of seats for women in legislative bodies.

concepts.name: Time Use Survey
concepts.definition: A survey that measures the time spent by people on various activities.

concepts.name: Census of India
concepts.definition: A survey that counts the population of India.

concepts.name: National Sample Survey
concepts.definition: A survey that collects data on various aspects of Indian society.

concepts.name: Urban-Rural Divide
concepts.definition: A concept that refers to the differences between urban and rural areas in India.

concepts.name: Economic Inequality
concepts.definition: A concept that refers to the unequal distribution of resources and opportunities in India.

concepts.name: Resources
concepts.definition: Everything available in our environment which can be used to satisfy our needs, provided, it is technologically accessible, economically feasible and culturally acceptable can be termed as 'Resource'.

concepts.name: Classification of Resources
concepts.definition: Resources can be classified in the following ways- (a) On the basis of origin - biotic and abiotic (b) On the basis of exhaustibility - renewable and non-renewable (c) On the basis of ownership - individual, community, national and international (d) On the basis of status of development potential, developed stock and reserves.

concepts.name: Land as a Resource
concepts.definition: Land is a natural resource of utmost importance. It supports natural vegetation, wild life, human life, economic activities, transport and communication systems.

concepts.name: Land Degradation
concepts.definition: Human activities have not only brought about degradation of land but have also aggravated the pace of natural forces to cause damage to land.

concepts.name: Soil as a Resource
concepts.definition: Soil is the most important renewable natural resource. It is the medium of plant growth and supports different types of living organisms on the earth.

concepts.name: Classification of Soils
concepts.definition: Soils of India are classified in different types on the basis of the factors responsible for soil formation, colour, thickness, texture, age, chemical and physical properties.

concepts.name: Alluvial Soils
concepts.definition: Alluvial soil is the most widely spread and important soil. It has been deposited by three important Himalayan river systems- the Indus, the Ganga and the Brahmaputra.

concepts.name: Black Soil
concepts.definition: Black soil is ideal for growing cotton and is also known as black cotton soil. It is believed that climatic condition along with the parent rock material are the important factors for the formation of black soil.

concepts.name: Laterite Soil
concepts.definition: Laterite soil develops under tropical and subtropical climate with alternate wet and dry season. It is the result of intense leaching due to heavy rain.

concepts.name: Arid Soils
concepts.definition: Arid soils range from red to brown in colour. They are generally sandy in texture and saline in nature.

concepts.name: Forest Soils
concepts.definition: Forest soils are found in the hilly and mountainous areas where sufficient rain forests are available. The soils texture varies according to the mountain environment where they are formed.

concepts.name: Soil Erosion
concepts.definition: The denudation of the soil cover and subsequent washing down is described as soil erosion. The processes of soil formation and erosion, go on simultaneously and generally there is a balance between the two.

concepts.name: Conservation of Resources
concepts.definition: Resources are vital for any developmental activity. But irrational consumption and over-utilisation of resources may lead to socio-economic and environmental problems.

concepts.name: Sustainable Development
concepts.definition: Sustainable economic development means 'development should take place without damaging the environment, and development in the present should not compromise with the needs of the future generations.'

concepts.name: Resource Planning
concepts.definition: Resource planning is a complex process which involves : (i) identification and inventory of resources across the regions of the country. This involves surveying, mapping and qualitative and quantitative estimation and measurement of the resources.

concepts.name: Land Use Pattern in India
concepts.definition: The use of land is determined both by physical factors such as topography, climate, soil types as well as human factors such as population density, technological capability and culture and traditions etc.

concepts.name: Land Degradation and Conservation Measures
concepts.definition: There are many ways to solve the problems of land degradation. Afforestation and proper management of grazing can help to some extent.

concepts.name: Soil Formation
concepts.definition: Relief, parent rock or bed rock, climate, vegetation and other forms of life and time are important factors in the formation of soil.

concepts.name: Soil Erosion and Conservation
concepts.definition: The denudation of the soil cover and subsequent washing down is described as soil erosion. The processes of soil formation and erosion, go on simultaneously and generally there is a balance between the two.

concepts.name: Sustainable Resource Management
concepts.definition: Sustainable resource management is a means for resource conservation, which was introduced by the Brundtland Commission Report, 1987.

concepts.name: Minerals are an indispensable part of our lives
concepts.definition: Minerals are used in various forms in our daily life, from a tiny pin to a towering building or a big ship.

concepts.name: Minerals are extracted from the earth's crust
concepts.definition: Minerals are extracted from the earth's crust after proper refinement.

concepts.name: Minerals are used in various industries
concepts.definition: Minerals are used in various industries, including electrical, chemical, and metallurgical industries.

concepts.name: Minerals are essential for human life
concepts.definition: Minerals are essential for human life, and their deficiency can lead to various health problems.

concepts.name: Minerals are formed through geological processes
concepts.definition: Minerals are formed through geological processes, including the cooling and solidification of magma.

concepts.name: Minerals can be classified into different types
concepts.definition: Minerals can be classified into different types, including ferrous and non-ferrous minerals.

concepts.name: Ferrous minerals are used in the production of steel
concepts.definition: Ferrous minerals, such as iron ore, are used in the production of steel.

concepts.name: Non-ferrous minerals are used in various industries
concepts.definition: Non-ferrous minerals, such as copper and aluminum, are used in various industries, including electrical and chemical industries.

concepts.name: Minerals are found in different forms
concepts.definition: Minerals are found in different forms, including igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks.

concepts.name: Minerals can be extracted through different methods
concepts.definition: Minerals can be extracted through different methods, including open-pit mining and underground mining.

concepts.name: Coal is a major source of energy in India
concepts.definition: Coal is a major source of energy in India, and it is used in various industries, including power generation and steel production.

concepts.name: Petroleum is another major source of energy in India
concepts.definition: Petroleum is another major source of energy in India, and it is used in various industries, including transportation and power generation.

concepts.name: Natural gas is a clean and efficient source of energy
concepts.definition: Natural gas is a clean and efficient source of energy, and it is used in various industries, including power generation and transportation.

concepts.name: Solar energy is a renewable source of energy
concepts.definition: Solar energy is a renewable source of energy, and it is used in various industries, including power generation and water heating.

concepts.name: Wind energy is another renewable source of energy
concepts.definition: Wind energy is another renewable source of energy, and it is used in various industries, including power generation and water pumping.

concepts.name: Biomass is a renewable source of energy
concepts.definition: Biomass is a renewable source of energy, and it is used in various industries, including power generation and heat production.

concepts.name: Geothermal energy is a renewable source of energy
concepts.definition: Geothermal energy is a renewable source of energy, and it is used in various industries, including power generation and heat production.

concepts.name: Energy conservation is essential for sustainable development
concepts.definition: Energy conservation is essential for sustainable development, and it can be achieved through various means, including the use of renewable energy sources and energy-efficient technologies.

concepts.name: Mineral resources are finite and non-renewable
concepts.definition: Mineral resources are finite and non-renewable, and they need to be conserved for future generations.

concepts.name: Sustainable energy development is essential for the future
concepts.definition: Sustainable energy development is essential for the future, and it can be achieved through the use of renewable energy sources and energy-efficient technologies.

payload.name: Biodiversity

concepts.name: Height of a tower

concepts.name: Angle of elevation
concepts.definition: The angle formed by the line of sight with the horizontal when it is above the horizontal level.

concepts.name: Angle of depression

concepts.name: Line of sight

concepts.name: Right-angled triangle

concepts.name: Similar Polygons
concepts.definition: Two polygons of the same number of sides are similar, if (i) their corresponding angles are equal and (ii) their corresponding sides are in the same ratio (i.e., proportion).

concepts.name: Thales Theorem
concepts.definition: If a line is drawn parallel to one side of a triangle to intersect the other two sides in distinct points, then the other two sides are divided in the same ratio.

concepts.name: Basic Proportionality Theorem
concepts.definition: If a line divides any two sides of a triangle in the same ratio, then the line is parallel to the third side.

concepts.name: AAA Similarity Criterion
concepts.definition: If in two triangles, corresponding angles are equal, then their corresponding sides are in the same ratio and hence the two triangles are similar.

concepts.name: AA Similarity Criterion
concepts.definition: If in two triangles, two angles of one triangle are respectively equal to the two angles of the other triangle, then the two triangles are similar.

concepts.name: SSS Similarity Criterion
concepts.definition: If in two triangles, corresponding sides are in the same ratio, then their corresponding angles are equal and hence the triangles are similar.

concepts.name: SAS Similarity Criterion
concepts.definition: If one angle of a triangle is equal to one angle of another triangle and the sides including these angles are in the same ratio (proportional), then the triangles are similar.

concepts.name: Similarity of Triangles
concepts.definition: Two triangles are similar, if (i) their corresponding angles are equal and (ii) their corresponding sides are in the same ratio (or proportion).

concepts.name: Criteria for Similarity of Triangles

concepts.name: Nationalism
concepts.definition: A political ideology that emphasizes the importance of national identity and the nation-state.

concepts.name: Liberalism
concepts.definition: A political ideology that emphasizes individual freedom and equality before the law.

concepts.name: Conservatism
concepts.definition: A political ideology that emphasizes the importance of tradition and established institutions.

concepts.name: Revolution
concepts.definition: A sudden and violent overthrow of a government or social order.

concepts.name: Nation-state
concepts.definition: A state that is defined by a shared national identity and a clearly defined territory.

concepts.name: Liberal nationalism
concepts.definition: A political ideology that combines liberal and nationalist principles.

concepts.name: Romanticism
concepts.definition: A cultural movement that emphasizes the importance of emotions and intuition.

concepts.name: Folk culture
concepts.definition: The cultural practices and traditions of a particular region or nation.

concepts.name: Language
concepts.definition: A system of communication that is used by a particular group or nation.

concepts.name: National identity
concepts.definition: A sense of shared identity and belonging among a group of people.

concepts.name: Suffrage
concepts.definition: The right to vote in a political election.

concepts.name: Feminism
concepts.definition: A social and political movement that advocates for women's rights and equality.

concepts.name: Ideology
concepts.definition: A system of ideas that reflects a particular social and political vision.

concepts.name: Conservative order

concepts.name: Revolutionary nationalism

concepts.name: National Assembly

concepts.name: Why do organisms reproduce?
concepts.definition: Organisms reproduce to increase their population and ensure the survival of the species.

concepts.name: What is DNA copying?
concepts.definition: DNA copying is the process of creating a copy of the DNA molecule.

concepts.name: What is the importance of variation in reproduction?
concepts.definition: Variation is important in reproduction as it allows for the creation of new combinations of traits and increases the chances of survival.

concepts.name: What is the difference between binary fission and multiple fission?
concepts.definition: Binary fission involves the division of a cell into two equal daughter cells, while multiple fission involves the division of a cell into multiple daughter cells.

concepts.name: What is the role of the seminal vesicles and the prostate gland?
concepts.definition: The seminal vesicles and the prostate gland produce secretions that help to nourish and transport sperm.

concepts.name: What is the process of pollination?
concepts.definition: Pollination is the transfer of pollen from the anther to the stigma.

concepts.name: What is the process of fertilisation?
concepts.definition: Fertilisation is the fusion of the male and female gametes to form a zygote.

concepts.name: What is the difference between asexual and sexual reproduction?
concepts.definition: Asexual reproduction involves the creation of new individuals from a single parent, while sexual reproduction involves the combination of genetic material from two parents.

concepts.name: What is the role of the testes in human beings?
concepts.definition: The testes produce sperms and secrete testosterone.

concepts.name: What is the process of menstruation?
concepts.definition: Menstruation is the shedding of the uterine lining if fertilisation does not occur.

concepts.name: What is the purpose of the placenta?
concepts.definition: The placenta provides nourishment to the developing embryo.

concepts.name: What is the role of the vas deferens?
concepts.definition: The vas deferens transports sperm from the testes to the urethra.

concepts.name: What is the process of germination?
concepts.definition: Germination is the process by which a seed develops into a seedling.

concepts.name: What is the purpose of the stigma?
concepts.definition: The stigma receives pollen grains during pollination.

concepts.name: What is the role of the ovary?
concepts.definition: The ovary produces ovules that contain eggs.

concepts.name: What is the process of tissue culture?
concepts.definition: Tissue culture is the process of growing new plants from tissue or cells.

concepts.name: What is the purpose of the seminal vesicles?
concepts.definition: The seminal vesicles produce secretions that help to nourish and transport sperm.

concepts.name: What is the role of the prostate gland?
concepts.definition: The prostate gland produces secretions that help to nourish and transport sperm.

concepts.name: What is the process of budding?
concepts.definition: Budding is the process by which a new individual grows from a bud.

concepts.name: What is the purpose of the stigma?
concepts.definition: The stigma receives pollen grains during pollination.

concepts.name: Chemical Reactions and Equations
concepts.definition: A chemical reaction is a process in which one or more substances are converted into another substance or substances.

concepts.name: Balanced Chemical Equation
concepts.definition: A balanced chemical equation is a chemical equation in which the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides of the equation.

concepts.name: Combination Reaction
concepts.definition: A combination reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which two or more substances combine to form a new single substance.

concepts.name: Decomposition Reaction
concepts.definition: A decomposition reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which a single substance decomposes to give two or more substances.

concepts.name: Exothermic Reaction
concepts.definition: An exothermic reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which heat is given out along with the products.

concepts.name: Endothermic Reaction
concepts.definition: An endothermic reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which energy is absorbed.

concepts.name: Displacement Reaction
concepts.definition: A displacement reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which an element displaces another element from its compound.

concepts.name: Double Displacement Reaction
concepts.definition: A double displacement reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which two different atoms or groups of atoms (ions) are exchanged.

concepts.name: Precipitation Reaction
concepts.definition: A precipitation reaction is a type of chemical reaction in which a precipitate is formed.

concepts.name: Oxidation and Reduction
concepts.definition: Oxidation and reduction are types of chemical reactions in which a substance gains or loses oxygen or hydrogen.

concepts.name: Corrosion
concepts.definition: Corrosion is the process of a metal being attacked by substances around it, such as moisture, acids, etc.

concepts.name: Rancidity
concepts.definition: Rancidity is the process of fats and oils becoming oxidised and changing their smell and taste.

concepts.name: Exothermic and Endothermic Reactions
concepts.definition: Exothermic and endothermic reactions are types of chemical reactions in which heat is given out or absorbed.

concepts.name: Respiration
concepts.definition: Respiration is an exothermic process in which glucose combines with oxygen to produce energy.

concepts.name: Combination and Decomposition Reactions
concepts.definition: Combination and decomposition reactions are types of chemical reactions in which substances combine or decompose to form new substances.

concepts.name: Displacement and Double Displacement Reactions
concepts.definition: Displacement and double displacement reactions are types of chemical reactions in which elements or compounds are displaced or exchanged.

concepts.name: Precipitation and Oxidation-Reduction Reactions
concepts.definition: Precipitation and oxidation-reduction reactions are types of chemical reactions in which precipitates are formed or substances are oxidised or reduced.

concepts.name: Corrosion and Rancidity

concepts.definition: Biotic components comprise living organisms and abiotic components comprise physical factors like temperature, rainfall, wind, soil and minerals.

concepts.name: Producers
concepts.definition: Green plants and certain bacteria which can produce food by photosynthesis come under this category and are called the producers.

concepts.name: Consumers
concepts.definition: Organisms which consume the food produced, either directly from producers or indirectly by feeding on other consumers are the consumers.

concepts.name: Decomposers

concepts.definition: A series of organisms feeding on one another, forming a series or organisms taking part at various biotic levels.

concepts.name: Trophic Levels
concepts.definition: The autotrophs or the producers are at the first trophic level, herbivores or the primary consumers come at the second, small carnivores or the secondary consumers at the third and larger carnivores or the tertiary consumers form the fourth trophic level.

concepts.name: Energy Flow
concepts.definition: The flow of energy from one component of the system to another, from autotrophs to heterotrophs and decomposers.

concepts.name: Biodegradable Substances
concepts.definition: Substances that are broken down by biological processes are said to be biodegradable.

concepts.name: Non-Biodegradable Substances
concepts.definition: Substances that are not broken down in this manner are said to be non-biodegradable.

concepts.name: Ozone Layer
concepts.definition: Ozone (O 3 ) is a molecule formed by three atoms of oxygen, which shields the surface of the earth from ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the Sun.

concepts.name: CFCs
concepts.definition: Synthetic chemicals like chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs) which are used as refrigerants and in fire extinguishers.

concepts.name: Biological Magnification
concepts.definition: The phenomenon where harmful chemicals enter our bodies through the food chain.

concepts.name: Food Web
concepts.definition: A series of branching lines called a food web, showing the relationship between organisms.

concepts.name: Ecosystem Services
concepts.definition: The various components of an ecosystem are interdependent, providing services like producing food, water, air and climate regulation.

concepts.name: Waste Management
concepts.definition: The process of collecting, transporting, and disposing of waste in an environmentally responsible manner.

concepts.name: Biodegradable Materials
concepts.definition: Materials that can be broken down by microorganisms, such as paper, cotton, and food waste.

concepts.name: Non-Biodegradable Materials
concepts.definition: Materials that cannot be broken down by microorganisms, such as plastics, metals, and glass.

concepts.name: Environmental Impact
concepts.definition: The effects of human activities on the environment, including pollution, climate change, and loss of biodiversity.

concepts.name: Sustainable Development
concepts.definition: The practice of meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.

concepts.name: Control and Coordination
concepts.definition: Control and Coordination refers to the ability of living organisms to respond to changes in their environment and coordinate their movements.

concepts.name: Nervous System
concepts.definition: The nervous system is a complex network of nerve cells and nerve fibers that transmit signals throughout the body.

concepts.name: Reflex Action
concepts.definition: Reflex action is a rapid, automatic response to a stimulus that does not require conscious thought.

concepts.name: Voluntary Action
concepts.definition: Voluntary action is a deliberate and conscious movement of the body.

concepts.name: Involuntary Action
concepts.definition: Involuntary action is an unconscious movement of the body that is not under voluntary control.

concepts.name: Hormones
concepts.definition: Hormones are chemical messengers that are produced by endocrine glands and regulate various bodily functions.

concepts.name: Adrenaline
concepts.definition: Adrenaline is a hormone produced by the adrenal glands that prepares the body for 'fight or flight' responses.

concepts.name: Thyroxin
concepts.definition: Thyroxin is a hormone produced by the thyroid gland that regulates metabolism and growth.

concepts.name: Insulin
concepts.definition: Insulin is a hormone produced by the pancreas that regulates blood sugar levels.

concepts.name: Growth Hormone
concepts.definition: Growth hormone is a hormone produced by the pituitary gland that regulates growth and development.

concepts.name: Plant Hormones
concepts.definition: Plant hormones are chemical messengers that regulate plant growth and development.

concepts.name: Auxin
concepts.definition: Auxin is a plant hormone that promotes cell elongation and cell division.

concepts.name: Cytokinin
concepts.definition: Cytokinin is a plant hormone that promotes cell division and differentiation.

concepts.name: Abscisic Acid
concepts.definition: Abscisic acid is a plant hormone that inhibits cell growth and promotes dormancy.

concepts.name: Feedback Mechanism
concepts.definition: Feedback mechanism is a process by which the body regulates the release of hormones.

concepts.name: Endocrine Glands
concepts.definition: Endocrine glands are glands that produce hormones that regulate various bodily functions.

concepts.name: Nervous Tissue
concepts.definition: Nervous tissue is a type of tissue that is responsible for transmitting signals throughout the body.

concepts.name: Muscle Tissue
concepts.definition: Muscle tissue is a type of tissue that is responsible for movement and contraction.

concepts.name: Spinal Cord
concepts.definition: Spinal cord is a part of the central nervous system that transmits signals between the brain and the rest of the body.

concepts.name: Brain
concepts.definition: Brain is the main coordinating centre of the body that regulates various bodily functions.

concepts.name: Heredity
concepts.definition: The passing of traits from parents to offspring.

concepts.name: Variation
concepts.definition: The differences that occur in individuals within a species.

concepts.name: Reproduction
concepts.definition: The process by which living organisms produce offspring.

concepts.name: Independent Inheritance
concepts.definition: The process by which traits are inherited separately.

concepts.name: Dominant-Recessive
concepts.definition: The relationship between traits where one trait is dominant over the other.

concepts.name: Sex Determination
concepts.definition: The process by which the sex of an individual is determined.

concepts.name: Genetic Contribution
concepts.definition: The equal genetic contribution of male and female parents to the offspring.

concepts.name: Chromosomes
concepts.definition: The thread-like structures that carry genetic information.

concepts.name: Genes
concepts.definition: The basic units of heredity that carry genetic information.

concepts.name: Traits Expression
concepts.definition: The process by which traits are expressed in an individual.

concepts.name: Inheritance Pattern
concepts.definition: The pattern by which traits are inherited from parents to offspring.

concepts.name: Sex Chromosomes
concepts.definition: The chromosomes that determine the sex of an individual.

concepts.name: Genetic Information
concepts.definition: The information carried by genes and chromosomes.

concepts.name: Variation Survival
concepts.definition: The process by which variations in a species lead to increased survival.

concepts.name: Independent Inheritance of Traits
concepts.definition: The process by which traits are inherited independently.

concepts.name: Genetic Contribution of Parents
concepts.definition: The equal genetic contribution of male and female parents to the offspring.

concepts.name: Chromosomes and Genetic Information
concepts.definition: The relationship between chromosomes and genetic information.

concepts.name: Genes and Genetic Information
concepts.definition: The relationship between genes and genetic information.

concepts.name: Traits Expression and Genetic Information
concepts.definition: The relationship between traits expression and genetic information.

concepts.name: Inheritance Pattern and Genetic Information
concepts.definition: The relationship between inheritance pattern and genetic information.

concepts.name: Sex Chromosomes and Genetic Information
concepts.definition: The relationship between sex chromosomes and genetic information.

concepts.name: Genetic Information and Variation
concepts.definition: The relationship between genetic information and variation.

concepts.name: Independent Inheritance and Genetic Information
concepts.definition: The relationship between independent inheritance and genetic information.

concepts.name: Genetic Contribution and Genetic Information
concepts.definition: The relationship between genetic contribution and genetic information.

concepts.name: Acid-base indicators
concepts.definition: Dyes or mixtures of dyes used to indicate the presence of acids and bases.

concepts.name: Acidic nature of a substance
concepts.definition: Formation of H+ (aq) ions in solution.

concepts.name: Reaction of acid with metal
concepts.definition: Evolution of hydrogen gas and formation of a corresponding salt.

concepts.name: Reaction of base with metal
concepts.definition: Evolution of hydrogen gas and formation of a corresponding salt with a negative ion composed of the metal and oxygen.

concepts.name: Reaction of acid with metal carbonate or metal hydrogencarbonate
concepts.definition: Formation of a corresponding salt, carbon dioxide gas and water.

concepts.name: Acidic and basic solutions in water
concepts.definition: Conduct electricity due to the production of hydrogen and hydroxide ions.

concepts.name: pH scale
concepts.definition: A scale used to measure the hydrogen ion concentration in a solution.

concepts.name: Neutral solution
concepts.definition: A solution with a pH of exactly 7.

concepts.name: Acidic solution
concepts.definition: A solution with a pH less than 7.

concepts.name: Basic solution
concepts.definition: A solution with a pH more than 7.

concepts.name: Water of crystallisation
concepts.definition: The fixed number of water molecules present in one formula unit of a salt.

concepts.name: Salts and their uses
concepts.definition: Salts have various uses in everyday life and in industries.

concepts.name: pH change and its effects
concepts.definition: pH change can cause effects such as tooth decay, acid rain, and harm to living organisms.

concepts.name: Acid-base neutralisation
concepts.definition: Acids and bases neutralise each other to form corresponding salts and water.

concepts.name: pH of solutions
concepts.definition: pH of solutions can be measured using pH paper or pH meter.

concepts.name: pH of common substances
concepts.definition: pH of common substances such as saliva, lemon juice, and vinegar can be measured using pH paper or pH meter.

concepts.name: pH of soil
concepts.definition: pH of soil can be measured using pH paper or pH meter.

concepts.name: pH of digestive system
concepts.definition: pH of digestive system can be measured using pH paper or pH meter.

concepts.name: pH of tooth decay
concepts.definition: pH of tooth decay can be measured using pH paper or pH meter.

concepts.name: pH of Plaster of Paris
concepts.definition: pH of Plaster of Paris can be measured using pH paper or pH meter.

concepts.name: Times-tables

concepts.definition: Numbers that can be divided by 10 without leaving a remainder

concepts.definition: The process of finding the quotient of two numbers

concepts.name: Circle
concepts.definition: A circle is a set of all points in a plane that are 4 cm away from a fixed point called the centre.

concepts.name: Compass
concepts.definition: A compass is a tool used to draw circles and their parts.

concepts.name: Rectangle
concepts.definition: A rectangle is a 4-sided figure with opposite sides equal in length and all angles equal to 90°.

concepts.name: Square
concepts.definition: A square is a rectangle with all sides equal in length and all angles equal to 90°.

concepts.name: Diagonal
concepts.definition: A diagonal is a line that divides a rectangle into two triangles.

concepts.name: Opposite Sides
concepts.definition: Opposite sides of a rectangle are equal in length.

concepts.name: Right Angles
concepts.definition: Right angles are angles that measure 90°.

concepts.name: Construction
concepts.definition: Construction is the process of drawing a figure using a compass and a ruler.

concepts.name: Symmetry
concepts.definition: Symmetry is the property of a figure that remains the same when it is rotated or reflected.

concepts.name: Similarity
concepts.definition: Similarity is the property of two figures that have the same shape but not necessarily the same size.

concepts.name: Perpendicular
concepts.definition: A perpendicular is a line that is perpendicular to another line.

concepts.name: Radius
concepts.definition: The radius of a circle is the distance from the centre to any point on the circle.

concepts.name: Arc
concepts.definition: An arc is a part of a circle.

concepts.name: Angle
concepts.definition: An angle is a measure of the amount of rotation between two lines or planes.

concepts.name: Triangle
concepts.definition: A triangle is a 3-sided figure with three angles and three sides.

concepts.name: Line
concepts.definition: A line is a set of points that extend infinitely in two directions.

concepts.name: Point
concepts.definition: A point is a location in space.

concepts.name: Distance
concepts.definition: Distance is the length between two points.

concepts.name: Measurement
concepts.definition: Measurement is the process of finding the length of a line or the distance between two points.

concepts.name: Geometry
concepts.definition: Geometry is the study of shapes and their properties.

chapter_title: Chapter 1 The Wonderful World of Science
subject: science

concepts.name: Curiosity
concepts.definition: The desire to learn and understand the world around us.

concepts.name: Scientific Method
concepts.definition: A step-by-step process used to find answers to questions and solve problems.

concepts.name: Observation
concepts.definition: The act of paying attention to and noticing things around us.

concepts.name: Questioning
concepts.definition: Asking questions to understand and learn about the world.

concepts.name: Experimentation
concepts.definition: Testing and trying new things to find answers and solve problems.

concepts.name: Analysis
concepts.definition: Examining and interpreting data and results to draw conclusions.

concepts.name: Teamwork
concepts.definition: Working together with others to achieve common goals and solve problems.

concepts.name: Curiosity and Observation
concepts.definition: The combination of curiosity and observation to learn and understand the world.

concepts.name: Scientific Inquiry
concepts.definition: The process of asking questions and seeking answers through scientific methods.

concepts.name: Exploration
concepts.definition: The act of discovering and learning about new things and ideas.

concepts.name: Problem-Solving
concepts.definition: The process of identifying and solving problems using scientific methods.

concepts.name: Critical Thinking
concepts.definition: The ability to think critically and make informed decisions.

concepts.name: Science and Everyday Life
concepts.definition: The application of scientific principles and methods to everyday life.

concepts.name: Science and Technology
concepts.definition: The use of scientific principles and methods to develop new technologies and innovations.

concepts.name: The Scientific Method
concepts.definition: A systematic process used to find answers to questions and solve problems.

concepts.name: Observation and Measurement
concepts.definition: The process of observing and measuring things to gather data and information.

concepts.name: Data Analysis
concepts.definition: The process of examining and interpreting data to draw conclusions.

concepts.name: Science and Society
concepts.definition: The impact of science on society and the role of science in addressing societal issues.

concepts.name: Science and Ethics
concepts.definition: The consideration of ethical issues and principles in scientific research and practice.

concepts.name: Scientific Literacy
concepts.definition: The ability to understand and apply scientific principles and methods to everyday life.

chapter_title: Chapter 10 Living Creatures Exploring their Characteristics
subject: science

payload.name: Living and Non-Living
payload.definition: Things that are living can move, eat, grow, breathe, excrete, respond to stimuli, reproduce and die. Non-living things do not have these characteristics.

chapter_title: Chapter 2 Diversity in the Living World
subject: science

concepts.name: Diversity in Plants and Animals
concepts.definition: The variety of plants and animals found in a particular region contributes to the biodiversity of that region.

concepts.name: Grouping of Plants and Animals
concepts.definition: Grouping makes it easier to understand and study plants and animals on the basis of their similarities and differences.

concepts.name: Adaptation
concepts.definition: The special features that enable plants and animals to survive in a particular region are called adaptations.

concepts.name: Habitat
concepts.definition: The place where plants and animals live is their habitat.

concepts.name: Biodiversity
concepts.definition: The variety of plants and animals found in a particular region is a part of biodiversity.

concepts.name: Terrestrial Habitat
concepts.definition: The plants and animals that live on land are said to live in terrestrial habitats.

concepts.name: Aquatic Habitat
concepts.definition: The plants and animals that live in water are said to live in aquatic habitats.

concepts.name: Amphibians
concepts.definition: Some animals, such as frogs, can live in water as well as on land. These are called amphibians.

concepts.name: Dicot Plants
concepts.definition: Plants that have seeds with two cotyledons are called dicotyledons (dicots).

concepts.name: Monocot Plants
concepts.definition: Plants with seeds having one cotyledon are called monocotyledons (monocots).

concepts.name: Reticulate Venation
concepts.definition: A net-like pattern of veins on both sides of a thick middle vein is called reticulate venation.

concepts.name: Parallel Venation
concepts.definition: The veins run parallel in some leaves.

concepts.name: Taproot
concepts.definition: The main root of a plant is called a taproot.

concepts.name: Fibrous Root
concepts.definition: The roots of a plant that appear as a bunch of similar-sized thin roots arising from the base of the stem are called fibrous roots.

concepts.name: Herbs
concepts.definition: Some plants are typically small with soft and green stems. These are known as herbs.

concepts.name: Shrubs
concepts.definition: Some plants are not as tall as trees. These plants often have many brown woody stems that start branching very close to the ground. These plants are called shrubs.

concepts.name: Trees
concepts.definition: Some plants grow really tall and have hard, thick, brown, and woody stems. Their branches typically start higher up on the stem and away from the ground. These plants are called trees.

concepts.name: Climbers
concepts.definition: Some plants with weak stems need support to climb and grow, and are called climbers.

concepts.name: Crepers
concepts.definition: Some plants creep along the ground and are called creepers.

concepts.name: Sacred Groves
concepts.definition: Sacred groves are undisturbed patches of forests. Their sizes may vary from quite small to very large. Sacred groves are found all over India. They are home to different kinds of plants and animals, including numerous medicinal plants.

chapter_title: Chapter 3 Mindful Eating A Path to a Healthy Body
subject: science

concepts.name: Diversity in Food
concepts.definition: Food habits vary across states and regions, influenced by factors like cultivation of food crops, taste preferences, culture, and traditions.

concepts.name: Carbohydrates
concepts.definition: Carbohydrates are primary energy sources, found in foods like cereals, vegetables, and fruits.

concepts.name: Fats
concepts.definition: Fats are energy sources, found in foods like nuts, seeds, and oils.

concepts.name: Proteins
concepts.definition: Proteins are body-building nutrients, found in foods like milk, eggs, and meat.

concepts.name: Vitamins
concepts.definition: Vitamins are essential nutrients, found in foods like fruits, vegetables, and whole grains.

concepts.name: Minerals
concepts.definition: Minerals are essential nutrients, found in foods like milk, eggs, and meat.

concepts.name: Dietary Fibres
concepts.definition: Dietary fibres are essential components of food, found in foods like fruits, vegetables, and whole grains.

concepts.name: Balanced Diet
concepts.definition: A balanced diet provides all essential nutrients, roughage, and water in the right quantities for proper growth and development.

concepts.name: Food Miles
concepts.definition: Food miles refer to the distance travelled by a food item, from the place of its production to the consumer.

concepts.name: Junk Foods
concepts.definition: Junk foods are unhealthy, high in sugar and fat, but low in essential nutrients.

concepts.name: Millets
concepts.definition: Millets are nutrient-rich cereals, easily cultivated in different climatic conditions.

concepts.name: Culinary Practices
concepts.definition: Culinary practices have changed over time, influenced by factors like technology, transportation, and communication.

concepts.name: Food Components
concepts.definition: Food components are the building blocks of food, including carbohydrates, fats, proteins, vitamins, and minerals.

concepts.name: Nutrients
concepts.definition: Nutrients are essential components of food, providing energy, growth, and repair.

concepts.name: Roughage
concepts.definition: Roughage is essential for proper digestion and bowel movement.

concepts.name: Water
concepts.definition: Water is essential for proper digestion, absorption of nutrients, and removal of waste.

concepts.name: Food Safety
concepts.definition: Food safety refers to the regulation of food quality, including the addition of nutrients and the prevention of contamination.

concepts.name: Fortified Foods
concepts.definition: Fortified foods are foods that have added nutrients to improve their nutritional quality.

concepts.name: Iodised Salt
concepts.definition: Iodised salt is salt that has been fortified with iodine to prevent iodine deficiency.

concepts.name: Nutrition Research
concepts.definition: Nutrition research involves the study of the nutritional value of foods and the development of guidelines for a balanced diet.

payload.name: Temperature
payload.definition: The temperature of a body tells us how hot or cold it is.

concepts.name: Importance of Air
concepts.definition: Air is essential for our survival and is necessary for the growth of plants and animals.

concepts.name: Composition of Air
concepts.definition: Air is composed of 78% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, and 1% argon, carbon dioxide, and other gases.

concepts.name: Wind and Its Uses
concepts.definition: Wind is the movement of air and is used to generate electricity and power windmills.

concepts.name: Importance of Water
concepts.definition: Water is essential for our survival and is necessary for the growth of plants and animals.

concepts.name: Sources of Water
concepts.definition: Water is obtained from various sources such as rivers, lakes, and underground water.

concepts.name: Water Conservation
concepts.definition: Water conservation is necessary to prevent water pollution and ensure its availability for future generations.

concepts.name: Energy from the Sun
concepts.definition: The Sun is the main source of energy on Earth and is used for various purposes such as drying clothes and generating electricity.

concepts.name: Importance of Forests
concepts.definition: Forests are essential for our survival and provide food, shelter, and oxygen for plants and animals.

concepts.name: Soil Formation
concepts.definition: Soil is formed by the disintegration of rocks by actions of the Sun, water, and living organisms over a long time.

concepts.name: Importance of Rocks and Minerals
concepts.definition: Rocks and minerals are essential for our survival and are used for various purposes such as construction and manufacturing.

concepts.name: Fossil Fuels
concepts.definition: Fossil fuels are non-renewable resources that are formed from the remains of microorganisms and plants that got buried deep inside the earth.

concepts.name: Importance of Natural Resources
concepts.definition: Natural resources are essential for our survival and are necessary for the growth of plants and animals.

concepts.name: Classification of Natural Resources
concepts.definition: Natural resources can be classified as renewable and non-renewable resources.

concepts.name: Importance of Human-Made Resources
concepts.definition: Human-made resources are essential for our survival and are necessary for the growth of plants and animals.

concepts.name: Importance of Rainwater Harvesting
concepts.definition: Rainwater harvesting is an age-old practice in India and is necessary to conserve water and prevent water pollution.

concepts.name: Importance of Air Pollution Reduction
concepts.definition: Air pollution reduction is necessary to prevent health problems and ensure the availability of clean air for future generations.

concepts.name: Importance of Tree Plantation
concepts.definition: Tree plantation is necessary to conserve forests and prevent deforestation.

concepts.name: Importance of Water Conservation
concepts.definition: Water conservation is necessary to prevent water pollution and ensure its availability for future generations.

concepts.name: Importance of Energy Conservation
concepts.definition: Energy conservation is necessary to prevent energy depletion and ensure its availability for future generations.

concepts.name: Importance of Sustainable Living
concepts.definition: Sustainable living is necessary to conserve natural resources and prevent their depletion.

concepts.name: Stars
concepts.definition: Stars are massive, luminous spheres of plasma held together by their own gravity. They are primarily composed of hydrogen and helium, and generate energy through nuclear reactions in their cores.

concepts.name: Constellations
concepts.definition: Constellations are groups of stars that, when connected by imaginary lines, form patterns or shapes that resemble animals, objects, or mythological figures.

concepts.name: Pole Star
concepts.definition: The Pole Star, also known as Polaris, is a star located in the constellation Ursa Minor that remains stationary in the northern sky, serving as a navigational aid.

concepts.name: Sun
concepts.definition: The Sun is a star that is the center of our solar system, providing light and heat to the planets through nuclear reactions in its core.

concepts.name: Planets
concepts.definition: Planets are large, rocky or gaseous objects that orbit around the Sun, with their own atmospheres and potential for life.

concepts.name: Moon
concepts.definition: The Moon is Earth's natural satellite, orbiting the planet and affecting its tides and rotation.

concepts.name: Solar System
concepts.definition: The Solar System consists of the Sun, eight planets, their moons, and other smaller objects, including asteroids and comets, that orbit around the Sun.

concepts.name: Milky Way Galaxy
concepts.definition: The Milky Way Galaxy is a spiral galaxy that contains our Solar System, with hundreds of billions of stars and other celestial objects.

concepts.name: Universe
concepts.definition: The Universe is the vast expanse of all existence, containing countless galaxies, stars, and other celestial objects.

concepts.name: Asteroids
concepts.definition: Asteroids are small, rocky objects that orbit around the Sun, often found in the asteroid belt between Mars and Jupiter.

concepts.name: Comets
concepts.definition: Comets are icy bodies that release gas and dust as they approach the Sun, often producing a bright tail.

concepts.name: Satellites
concepts.definition: Satellites are objects that orbit around a larger body, such as a planet or moon, and can be natural or artificial.

concepts.name: Revolution
concepts.definition: Revolution refers to the movement of an object around a larger body, such as a planet or moon, in an elliptical or circular path.

concepts.name: Constellation boundaries
concepts.definition: Constellation boundaries are the regions of the sky that define the patterns of stars within a constellation.

concepts.name: International Astronomical Union
concepts.definition: The International Astronomical Union (IAU) is an organization that defines the official boundaries of constellations and maintains the official catalog of stars.

concepts.name: Sky mapping apps
concepts.definition: Sky mapping apps are software programs that help users identify stars, constellations, and planets in the night sky.

concepts.name: Telescope
concepts.definition: A telescope is an optical instrument that allows users to observe distant objects in the night sky, such as stars, planets, and galaxies.

concepts.name: Indian Institute of Astrophysics
concepts.definition: The Indian Institute of Astrophysics is a research institution that conducts studies on astronomy and astrophysics.

concepts.name: Hanle Dark Sky Reserve
concepts.definition: The Hanle Dark Sky Reserve is a protected area in Ladakh that is free from light pollution and offers a dark sky for stargazing.

concepts.name: Mount Saraswati
concepts.definition: Mount Saraswati is a mountain peak in Hanle, Ladakh, that has been renamed from Digparatsa-Ri and is home to the Indian Astronomical Observatory.

concepts.name: Proportional Reasoning
concepts.definition: Proportional reasoning is a mathematical concept that deals with the relationship between two or more quantities that change by the same factor.

concepts.name: Ratios
concepts.definition: Ratios are used to represent the relationship between two or more quantities.

concepts.name: Direct Proportions
concepts.definition: Direct proportions are used to represent the relationship between two or more quantities that change by the same factor.

concepts.name: Inverse Proportions
concepts.definition: Inverse proportions are used to represent the relationship between two or more quantities that change in opposite directions.

concepts.name: Representative Fraction
concepts.definition: A representative fraction is an expression that shows the ratio between a distance on a map and the corresponding actual distance on the ground.

concepts.name: Pie Chart
concepts.definition: A pie chart is a circular chart that shows different proportions of a whole.

concepts.name: Cross-Multiplication
concepts.definition: Cross-multiplication is a method used to verify if two ratios are proportional.

concepts.name: Map Scale
concepts.definition: A map scale is a ratio that shows the relationship between a distance on a map and the corresponding actual distance on the ground.

concepts.name: Dividing a Whole
concepts.definition: Dividing a whole in a given ratio means dividing the whole into parts in the ratio given.

concepts.name: Triangle Angles
concepts.definition: The sum of the angles in a triangle is 180°.

concepts.name: Inverse Proportionality
concepts.definition: Inverse proportionality is a relationship between two or more quantities that change in opposite directions.

concepts.name: Direct Proportionality
concepts.definition: Direct proportionality is a relationship between two or more quantities that change by the same factor.

concepts.name: Ratio Simplification

chapter_title: Chapter 9 The Amazing World of Solutes, Solvents, and Solutions
subject: science

concepts.id: 9.1 What Are Solute, Solvent, and Solution?
concepts.name: What Are Solute, Solvent, and Solution?
concepts.definition: A uniform mixture, such as that of salt or sugar, and water, is called a solution. Whenever a solid is mixed with a liquid to form a solution, the solid component is called the solute, and the liquid component is called the solvent.

concepts.id: 9.2 How Much Solute Can a Fixed Amount of Solvent Dissolve?
concepts.name: How Much Solute Can a Fixed Amount of Solvent Dissolve?
concepts.definition: The maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a fixed quantity of the solvent is called its solubility.

concepts.name: Solubility of Gases
concepts.definition: Many gases, including oxygen, dissolve in water. Oxygen dissolves in water only to a small extent. Even though present in minute quantities, it is this dissolved oxygen that sustains all aquatic life, including plants, fishes, and other organisms.

concepts.id: 9.4 Why Do Objects Float or Sink in Water?
concepts.name: Why Do Objects Float or Sink in Water?
concepts.definition: You must have observed that some objects float while others sink in water. You may have noticed that, while washing rice, husk particles present in the rice float on the surface of water while rice sinks to the bottom of the container. Why does this happen? If you add oil to water, it floats on water. Generally, it is believed that objects that float in a liquid are lighter and others that sink are heavier than the liquid.

concepts.name: What Is Density?
concepts.definition: Density is defined as the mass present in a unit volume of that substance. The density of a substance may be expressed mathematically using the formula: Density = Mass Volume.

concepts.name: Determination of density
concepts.definition: The density of an object can be determined by measuring its mass and volume.

concepts.name: Effect of temperature on density
concepts.definition: Generally, the density of a substance decreases with heating and increases with cooling.

concepts.name: Effect of pressure on density
concepts.definition: Pressure affects density differently depending on the state of matter.

concepts.id: 9.5.4 Why does ice float on water?
concepts.name: Why does ice float on water?
concepts.definition: Ice floats on water because it is lighter than liquid water.

concepts.id: 9.6 What happens when you add too much sugar to your tea and it stops dissolving?
concepts.name: What happens when you add too much sugar to your tea and it stops dissolving?
concepts.definition: When you add too much sugar to your tea and it stops dissolving, it forms a saturated solution. A saturated solution is a solution in which the maximum amount of solute has been dissolved, and no more of it can be dissolved at that temperature.

concepts.id: 9.7 Why does sugar and salt dissolve in water but not in oil?
concepts.name: Why does sugar and salt dissolve in water but not in oil?
concepts.definition: Sugar and salt dissolve in water because water is a good solvent. Water is a good solvent because it has a high degree of hydrogen bonding, which allows it to dissolve polar substances like sugar and salt. Oil, on the other hand, is a poor solvent because it does not have hydrogen bonding, and therefore cannot dissolve polar substances.

concepts.id: 9.8 Why are water bottles usually tall and cylindrical in shape instead of spherical?
concepts.name: Why are water bottles usually tall and cylindrical in shape instead of spherical?
concepts.definition: Water bottles are usually tall and cylindrical in shape because it allows for the maximum volume of water to be stored in a given surface area. This is due to the fact that the volume of a cylinder is greater than that of a sphere of the same surface area.

concepts.id: 9.9 Why does the level of a coloured liquid in a measuring cylinder coincide with the top of the meniscus?
concepts.name: Why does the level of a coloured liquid in a measuring cylinder coincide with the top of the meniscus?
concepts.definition: The level of a coloured liquid in a measuring cylinder coincides with the top of the meniscus because the meniscus is the curved surface of the liquid in the measuring cylinder, and it is the top of the meniscus that is used to measure the volume of the liquid.

concepts.id: 9.10 Why does the density of a substance decrease with an increase in temperature?
concepts.name: Why does the density of a substance decrease with an increase in temperature?
concepts.definition: The density of a substance decreases with an increase in temperature because the particles of the substance move faster and spread out, increasing the volume of the substance.

concepts.id: 9.11 Why does the density of a substance increase with a decrease in temperature?
concepts.name: Why does the density of a substance increase with a decrease in temperature?
concepts.definition: The density of a substance increases with a decrease in temperature because the particles of the substance move slower and come closer together, decreasing the volume of the substance.

concepts.id: 9.12 Why does the density of a substance change with a change in pressure?
concepts.name: Why does the density of a substance change with a change in pressure?
concepts.definition: The density of a substance changes with a change in pressure because the particles of the substance are affected by the pressure, causing the volume of the substance to change.

concepts.name: Why does the density of a substance change with a change in temperature?
concepts.definition: The density of a substance changes with a change in temperature because the particles of the substance move faster or slower, causing the volume of the substance to change.

concepts.definition: India is a vast country with varied land forms, including mountains, plains, deserts, plateaus, and islands.

concepts.name: Poverty as a Challenge
concepts.definition: Poverty is a multidimensional problem that affects many aspects of life, including income, education, health, and living standards.

concepts.definition: Landlessness is a major cause of poverty, as it limits access to resources and opportunities.

concepts.name: Unemployment
concepts.definition: Unemployment is a major cause of poverty, as it limits access to income and resources.

concepts.name: Size of Families
concepts.definition: Large families can be a cause of poverty, as they increase the burden on resources and income.

concepts.name: Literacy Levels

concepts.name: Health/Nutrition

concepts.name: Helplessness

concepts.name: Vulnerability

concepts.name: Poverty Estimates

concepts.name: Poverty Reduction

chapter_title: Chapter 5 India a Home to Many

concepts.name: Indian Values

concepts.name: Cultural Exchange

chapter_title: Chapter 3 The Rise of the Marathas
subject: class_8th_social_science

concepts.name: Marathas
concepts.definition: A group of people native to the Deccan plateau, identified with the language they speak, Marathi, which has had a rich and continuous literary history since the 12th century.

concepts.name: Shivaji
concepts.definition: A master strategist and a true visionary, born in 1630, who rose to power and became the Chhatrapati of the Maratha kingdom, known for his military campaigns and guerrilla warfare tactics.

concepts.name: Maratha Navy
concepts.definition: A revolutionary step taken by Shivaji to establish a navy to secure access to the resources of coastal regions, which became legendary for its exploits.

concepts.name: Bhakti
concepts.definition: A path of devotion to the divine or a particular deity, which was popular in India between the 7th and 17th centuries, and was characterized by devotional songs and poetry in the languages of the masses.

concepts.name: Jāgīr
concepts.definition: A piece of land given by a king or ruler to someone as a reward for their service, which could be used to collect taxes and support the king's needs.

concepts.name: Swarājya
concepts.definition: A vision of a sovereign kingdom or self-rule, which was promoted by Shivaji and the Marathas, emphasizing political, economic, and cultural aspects.

concepts.name: Guerrilla warfare
concepts.definition: A military tactic that uses small groups of people in a focused way, with speed, surprise, and knowledge of the terrain to defeat bigger armies.

concepts.name: Surgical strike
concepts.definition: A military attack intended to damage only a specific target, with no or minimal damage to other people or infrastructure.

concepts.name: Maratha administration
concepts.definition: A relatively centralised administration instituted by Shivaji, which abolished hereditary posts and land assignments, and paid a salary to every government official out of the state treasury.

concepts.name: Chauth
concepts.definition: A tax levied by the Marathas on provinces that were not directly under them, which was 25% of the revenue collected.

concepts.name: Sardeshmukhi
concepts.definition: An additional 10% tax levied by the Marathas on provinces that were not directly under them, on top of the chauth tax.

concepts.name: Maratha Navy's maritime supremacy
concepts.definition: The Maratha Navy's ability to resist European naval supremacy despite lack of access to the latest technology of the time.

concepts.name: Maratha women
concepts.definition: Maratha women who played significant roles in Maratha history, such as Tarabai and Ahilyabai Holkar, who were brave warriors and administrators.

concepts.name: Maratha legacy
concepts.definition: The lasting impact of the Marathas on Indian history, including their challenge to Mughal dominance, establishment of a new way of governing, and revival of local Hindu traditions.

concepts.name: Maratha administration's judicial system
concepts.definition: A judicial system that was efficient and moderate in using capital punishment, with a panchāyat as the main body that delivered justice.

concepts.name: Maratha trade networks
concepts.definition: The Marathas' involvement in maritime foreign trade, including the construction of roads and maintenance of ferries for riverine transport.

concepts.name: Maratha cultural revival
concepts.definition: The Marathas' contribution to India's cultural developments, including the promotion of the Marathi language, Sanskrit, and traditional arts.

concepts.name: Maratha forts
concepts.definition: The strategic importance of Maratha forts, which were the core of the Maratha state and played a crucial role in their survival against larger enemies.

concepts.name: Maratha expansion
concepts.definition: The Marathas' expansion into new territories, including the conquest of the South and the establishment of a pan-Indian empire.

concepts.name: Maratha women's contributions
concepts.definition: The significant contributions of Maratha women, such as Tarabai and Ahilyabai Holkar, to Maratha history and culture.

concepts.name: Sub-atomic particles
concepts.definition: The particles that make up an atom are called sub-atomic particles.

concepts.name: Electrons
concepts.definition: Electrons are negatively charged sub-atomic particles.

concepts.name: Protons
concepts.definition: Protons are positively charged sub-atomic particles.

concepts.name: Neutrons
concepts.definition: Neutrons are neutral sub-atomic particles.

concepts.name: Atomic number
concepts.definition: The number of protons in an atom is called the atomic number.

concepts.name: Mass number
concepts.definition: The sum of the number of protons and neutrons in an atom is called the mass number.

concepts.name: Isotopes
concepts.definition: Atoms of the same element with different mass numbers are called isotopes.

concepts.name: Isobars
concepts.definition: Atoms of different elements with the same mass number are called isobars.

concepts.name: Valency
concepts.definition: The combining capacity of an atom is called valency.

concepts.name: Bohr's model of the atom
concepts.definition: Bohr's model of the atom proposes that electrons are distributed in different shells with discrete energy around the nucleus.

concepts.name: Rutherford's model of the atom
concepts.definition: Rutherford's model of the atom proposes that a very tiny nucleus is present inside the atom and electrons revolve around this nucleus.

concepts.name: J.J. Thomson's model of the atom
concepts.definition: J.J. Thomson's model of the atom proposes that electrons are embedded in a positive sphere.

concepts.name: Atomic number and mass number
concepts.definition: The atomic number and mass number of an atom are related to the number of protons and neutrons in its nucleus.

concepts.name: Isotopes and isobars

concepts.name: Valency and electron configuration
